task: I need to send a quick note to my supervisor about a schedule change that came up, and I want it to be clear enough to read fast. Can you help me put it together?

prefs.short: Prefers plain, respectful talk over polished formality
prefs.explanation: He comes across as practical and unpretentious, so overly formal language would likely feel stiff. A respectful, straightforward tone fits his outlook better than anything flashy or corporate. He seems more comfortable with everyday language than with performative politeness.

prefs.short: Wants answers that stay concise and to the point
prefs.explanation: His life is built around getting work done efficiently, so he likely values responses that do not ramble. Short, useful answers would fit his practical style better than long explanations. He probably only wants more detail when it directly helps him solve something.

He seems to prefer straightforward communication that gets to the point without extra hedging. His practical mindset suggests he would rather hear what matters than wade through softened language. Directness would likely feel more honest and useful to him.

prefs.short: Best served with simple bullets or checklists
prefs.explanation: He likely benefits from information that is easy to scan and act on quickly. Bullets or short checklists would match his work-focused, hands-on style better than dense prose. He seems like someone who would appreciate a clear sequence of steps when needed.

prefs.short: Prefers plain English, not technical wording
prefs.explanation: He does not seem to want jargon or fancy phrasing when asking for help. Plain English would fit his practical, grounded way of dealing with problems. If the wording gets too technical, it would probably feel unnecessary or tiring to him.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a clear, quick note you can send:

**Hi [Supervisor’s Name],**  
I wanted to let you know about a schedule change that came up. [Briefly explain the change]. I wanted to give you a heads-up as soon as possible. Please let me know if you’d like me to adjust anything.  

If you want, I can also make it:
- more formal
- more casual
- shorter/text-message style

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful language like "here’s a clear, quick note" and "Hi [Supervisor’s Name]," without sounding flashy or corporate. Slightly polished, but still straightforward.
Keeps it concise and to the point: a short template plus brief options. It avoids rambling and focuses on what the user needs.
Very direct: "I wanted to let you know about a schedule change" and "Please let me know if you’d like me to adjust anything" get straight to the point with minimal hedging.
Uses a simple, easy-to-scan format with a short drafted message and bullet options for variations. Not a checklist, but still clean and scannable.
Uses plain English throughout, with no jargon or technical wording. Phrases like "give you a heads-up" and "shorter/text-message style" are everyday language.

turns.agent_response:
Sure — here’s a short, plain version you can send right now:

Hi [Supervisor’s Name], I need to let you know my schedule changed and I won’t be able to make [shift/day] as planned. I’m sorry for the short notice. Please let me know if you want me to switch shifts or make up the hours another day.

If you want, I can also make it a little more formal or more casual.

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful talk like "Hi [Supervisor’s Name]" and "I’m sorry for the short notice," without sounding stiff or corporate.
Keeps it concise and to the point: "here’s a short, plain version" and a brief note that can be sent right away.
Very direct: "my schedule changed and I won’t be able to make [shift/day] as planned" gets straight to the issue with no extra hedging.
It is easy to scan, but it is presented as a short paragraph rather than bullets or a checklist, so it only partially matches the preference.
Uses plain English and avoids jargon or technical wording; phrases like "switch shifts" and "make up the hours" are simple and practical.
